description: Trigger and behavior cases for audit log design: taxonomy completeness, append-only integrity, explicit write-failure policy, tenant-scoped access, negative tests.

cases.prompt: We're going for SOC 2 and our 'audit log' is currently console.log statements. Design a real audit system for the platform.

assertions: Builds an event taxonomy across authentication, access-control changes, data access/export, admin/support actions, security events, and billing — with explicit exclusions rather than silent gaps.
Defines a versioned record schema with resolvable actor identity and on-behalf-of for impersonation.
Makes append-only a store-permission property and answers the write-failure (fail-open vs fail-closed) question per category.
Delivers negative tests: cross-tenant audit reads denied, tamper attempts fail, and action-without-event completeness probes.

cases.prompt: Compliance wants every document view audited, but we serve two million views a day. How should the audit design handle that?

assertions: Treats grain coarsening of read events as an explicit, compliance-signed decision — not silent sampling.
Keeps writes and access-control changes at full grain regardless of volume.
Addresses storage via time partitioning with retention jobs as the only sanctioned deletion path.

cases.prompt: Design the events our partner integrations subscribe to when invoices are created or projects change.

assertions: Integration-consumed event contracts are api-event-architect's job; an audit trail is an internal record, not a subscription feed.

cases.prompt: When a tenant is purged, should their audit history be destroyed too, or kept? Set up whatever's standard.

assertions: Refuses to resolve the purge-vs-retention tension silently by 'standard' — it is a legal decision.
Presents both options (destroy with the tenant vs retain de-identified/platform-scoped) with consequences and routes the choice through human-approval-boundary.
